coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres: A simple platform for Cr(VI) detection in environmental water
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Meilin
Li, Taotao
Zhang, Cheng
Zheng, Yu
Wu, Chenqing
Zhang, Jian
Zhang, Kui
Zhang, Zhongping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, amino-functionalized mesoporous silica nanospheres (NH2-mSiO2) anchored with carbon dots (CDs) have been designed to construct an outstanding fluorescent sensor for heavy metal detection. Uniform mSiO2 was chosen to provide an optically transparent scaffold for immobilizing CDs. With the help of amino group modification on the surface of silica, benzene-1,4-diboronic acid (BA) was used as raw material to load CDs in the pores of mSiO2 by one-step solvothermal method. The proposed nanohybrid can solve the problem of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, leading to bright blue emission at 450 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of NH2-mSiO2@CDs showed high sensitivity to Cr(VI) in acetic acid buffer solution (pH = 4) with detection limit as low as 5 nM by inner filter effect (IFE) and electrostatic interaction (EI). The proposed method can also be extended to other CDs-based detection systems for chemical/biological sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mesoporous silica
Inner filter effect
Fluorometry
Hexavalent chromium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of amino-terminated mesoporous silica spheres (NH2-mSiO2)
Determination of Cr(VI) in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21675158 and 21976002 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province ( 1908085MB41 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of fluorescent NH2-mSiO2@CDs. (b-d) TEM images of mSiO2, NH2-mSiO2 and NH2-mSiO2@CDs, respectively. Insets showed the photographs of mSiO2, NH2-mSiO2 and NH2-mSiO2@CDs dispersion under 365 nm UV lamp, respectively.

(a) TEM image of the CDs obtained by corrosion of mSiO2 nanosphere. (b) HR-TEM image of the CDs. (c) XPS full survey of the CDs. (d) UV–vis absorbance spectra of the NH2-mSiO2 and NH2-mSiO2@CDs. (e) TGA curves of NH2-mSiO2 and NH2-mSiO2@CDs from room temperature to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NH2-mSiO2@CDs with different excitation wavelength. (b) Fluorescence emission intensities of the NH2-mSiO2@CDs at different pH values in acetic acid buffer solutions. (c) Fluorescence emission intensity changes of NH2-mSiO2@CDs over 30 days. (d) The photographs of NH2-mSiO2@CDs dispersed in water (left), after centrifugal separation (middle), and redispersed in water (right).

(a) Fluorescence quenching of NH2-mSiO2@CDs by Cr(VI) (10 μM) at different pH values. (b) Time course of fluorescence emission intensity in the presence of 5 and 10 μM Cr(VI) in 60 min (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of NH2-mSiO2@CDs in acetic acid buffer solution (pH = 4) with different amounts of Cr(VI) under 340 nm excitation. (d) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and concentration of Cr(VI).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) and NH2-mSiO2@CDs in buffer (pH = 4), and photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of NH2-mSiO2@CDs. (b) PL lifetime measurement of NH2-mSiO2@CDs in the presence and absence of Cr(VI).
The selectivity of NH2-mSiO2@CDs toward various interfering analytes. The concentration of the interfering analyte is 10 μM.
Comparison of fluorescence assay for detection of Cr(VI).
Recovery test of proposed method for Cr(VI) in lake water.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres: A simple platform for Cr(VI) detection in environmental water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan, Anhui 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan, Anhui 243032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Physical Science and Information Technology, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, amino-functionalized mesoporous silica nanospheres (NH2-mSiO2) anchored with carbon dots (CDs) have been designed to construct an outstanding fluorescent sensor for heavy metal detection. Uniform mSiO2 was chosen to provide an optically transparent scaffold for immobilizing CDs. With the help of amino group modification on the surface of silica, benzene-1,4-diboronic acid (BA) was used as raw material to load CDs in the pores of mSiO2 by one-step solvothermal method. The proposed nanohybrid can solve the problem of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching, leading to bright blue emission at 450 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of NH2-mSiO2@CDs showed high sensitivity to Cr(VI) in acetic acid buffer solution (pH = 4) with detection limit as low as 5 nM by inner filter effect (IFE) and electrostatic interaction (EI). The proposed method can also be extended to other CDs-based detection systems for chemical/biological sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal contamination has always been a widespread concern around the world due to the toxicity, persistence, and bioaccumulation in the soil environment, surface water, and even food security (Zheng et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017b; Wang et al., 2019a, 2020a). Chromium, a hazardous heavy metal, has been widely used in many fields such as metal plating, ink manufacture, leather tanning, and textile industries. Chromium-containing effluents and solid wastes released from the factories may cause major health hazards because hexavalent chromium is mutagenicity and carcinogenicity to living organisms (Luo et al., 2021). Thus, Cr(VI) has been defined as a“Group A Carcinogen” by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (Dhal et al., 2013; Kim et al., 2015). Therefore, it is of great significance to detect Cr(VI) in the environment. In the past few decades, various methods have been developed for the detection of Cr(VI), such as colorimetry (Ravindran et al., 2012), atomic absorption spectroscopy (Kiran et al., 2018), electrochemical technique (Korshoj et al., 2015), spectrophotometry (Ohira et al., 2017), and fluorometry (Tian et al., 2017; Qiu et al., 2020). Among them, the fluorescence method is superior due to its simplicity, convenience, and high sensitivity.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging class of zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials (typically less than 10 nm) with many unique properties and have been attracted much interest in recent years (Georgakilas et al., 2015; Chu et al., 2020). CDs exhibit outstanding advantages, including tunable photoluminescence, robust photostability, excellent electron acceptor/donor characteristics, and easy preparation (Feng et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2016). Depend on these benefits, CDs have been suggested for a wide array of applications, such as lighting, catalyst, and chem/biosensing (Wang et al., 2015; Rosso et al., 2020; Chan et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). In particular, the fluorescent properties of CDs have shown great promising applications in hazardous substance detection according to the change of luminescence (Ma et al., 2020; Kumari and Sahu 2020). However, there are several critical challenges for CDs-based fluorescence sensors. i), The purification of CDs is very troublesome. For example, using dialysis bags to purify CDs is a very time-consuming and water-consuming process (Zhou et al., 2019) ii), The surface groups of the CDs depend on the choice of precursors (Dhenadhayalan et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019). iii), Self-quenching is one of the significant problems (Zhang et al., 2020a).

To alleviate these shortcomings, many scientists had studied the use of additional substrates to enhance the performance of CDs (Zhang et al., 2020b), such as mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (Wang et al., 2019b), zeolitic crystalline matrices (Liu et al., 2017b), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) (Li et al., 2020), two-dimensional layered germinate (Liu et al., 2018), nanosheets (Zhang et al., 2019a), silica nanoparticles (Ryplida et al., 2019) and so on. For instance, a nanoplatform was designed via modifying mesoporous carbon nanoparticles by loading doxorubicin with tumor-targeting CDs that have simultaneous multifunctional applications for photothermal therapy, chemotherapy, and imaging (Wang et al., 2019b). A new class of CDs-based thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) material was reported by in situ embedding CDs within a series of zeolitic crystalline matrices under solvothermal or hydrothermal conditions. The CDs@zeolite composites showed high quantum yields up to 52.14% and ultralong TADF lifetimes up to 350 ms at ambient temperature and atmosphere (Liu et al., 2017b). Therefore, selecting of the material used as a matrice is crucial to highlight the optical properties and overcome some adverse factors of CDs.

Mesoporous silica nanospheres (mSiO2) have emerged as one of the most robust scaffolds for delivery applications due to their distinctive characteristics such as tailored mesoporous structure, high surface area, superior biocompatibility, better transmittance and facile surface functionalization (Nairi et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2015; Jia et al., 2017). Recently, mSiO2 cooperated with various kinds of modifications have been fabricated as multifunctional nanomaterials and demonstrated great potential in drug storage or delivery (Manzano and Vallet-Regí, 2019; Lee et al., 2020). It also has been combined with many nanomaterials to form nanoplatforms, such as quantum dots (Wang et al., 2020b), lanthanide nanoparticles (Wang et al., 2017), and noble metal nanoclusters (Meng et al., 2019), which are used for detection and imaging in vitro or in vivo. For example, a detection system that was encapsulated AuAg nanoclusters (NCs) into the pore channels of the mSiO2 via self-assembly was fabricated, which delivered AuAg NCs into living cells and made an efficient probe for Cu2+ sensing and imaging (Meng et al., 2019). In addition, mSiO2 is not only a good carrier but also a miniature reactor, which provides restricted space by the dimension of the silica pores (Cao et al., 2018). The different pore sizes mesoporous silica nanoparticles were used as template to synthesize the size-controlled Gd@CDs in one-step, which could avoid time-consuming purification and size enrichment (Chen et al., 2016). Inspired by this, we used mesoporous silica nanospheres as the scaffold to cooperate with CDs to form mSiO2@CDs for heavy metal detection.

In this work, a novel fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) was fabricated by solvothermal synthesis of luminescent CDs in the channel of mSiO2. The amino modified mSiO2 nanospheres were employed as the scaffold, while benzene-1,4-diboronic acid (BA) were used as the carbon source to form blue luminescent CDs. The NH2-mSiO2@CDs exhibited uniform morphology and excellent dispersibility. In the presence of Cr(VI), the fluorescent emission of NH2-mSiO2 @CDs was significantly quenched with linear relationship from 0.05 to 9 μM. Moreover, the developed sensing platform was successfully applied to lake water with satisfactory results, showing high potential for specific recognition and accurate quantification of Cr(VI) in complex matrices.

Triethanolamine (TEA), sodium salicylate (NaSal), benzene-1,4-diboronic acid (BA), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), potassium chromate (K2CrO4), chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate, cadmium chloride, nickel chloride hexahydrate, zinc sulfate heptahydrate, lead nitrate, cobalt chloride hexahydrate, iron(III) chloride hexahydrate, magnesium chloride hexahydrate, ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate, copper(II) chloride dehydrate and humic acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEEA) was obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), thiourea (Th), acetate (HAc), sodium acetate anhydrous (NaAc), ethanol and methanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All of the chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amino-terminated mesoporous silica spheres (NH2-mSiO2)
ce:section.ce:para: The mSiO2 was prepared based on previous publications with minor modifications (Yang et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2018). Firstly, 68 mg of TEA was dissolved into 25 mL of water and stirred (450 rpm/min) at 80 °C in an oil bath for 30 min. Then, 380 mg of CTAB and 168 mg of NaSal were added to the above solution and stirred for another 1 h. Subsequently, the solution was injected with 4 mL of TEOS and kept stirring for 2 h under the same condition. After that, a milky liquid was obtained, which was mixed with ethanol and centrifuged (10,000 rpm/min) to obtain a white transparent precipitate. The precipitate was washed several times with ethanol and finally dispersed in HCl/methanol mixture (V/V = 1:10) and refluxed at 80 °C for 6 h to extract the organic residue templates. The extraction was repeated once and the final product was dispersed in 100 mL of ethanol for amino group modification.

In order to prepare amino-terminated mSiO2, 1 mL of AEEA was added into the above mSiO2 ethanol dispersion, which was stirred vigorously at room temperature for 24 h. The obtained product was washed with ethanol and finally dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol for further use.

25 mg of BA was added into 6 mL of the above prepared amino-terminated mSiO2 ethanol dispersion, and the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 4 h to make the BA fill the mesoporous channels. After the incubation, the excessive BA was removed by centrifugation for 10 min (10,000 rpm) and the precipitation was re-dispersed in 6 mL of ethanol. The above dispersion was transferred into a 10 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and processed under 200 °C for 8 h. The synthesized NH2-mSiO2@CDs were cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged to remove free CDs. The product was dispersed in 15 mL of ethanol (2.5 mg/mL) for subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) was performed in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4) at room temperature. Typically, a series of K2CrO4 solutions with different concentrations were added to 1 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer with 30 μL of NH2-mSiO2@CDs dispersion in 1 cm quartz cuvette. After mixed evenly, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

To examine the selectivity, the fluorescence of NH2-mSiO2@CDs was investigated against Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cr(III), Cd2+, Ni2+ and Pb2+ at 10 μM. For anti-interference test, the fluorescence quenching by 10 μM of Cr(VI) were recorded in the presence of SO4 2− (100 μM), PO4 3− (100 μM), Cl− (1 mM), NO3 − (100 μM), and humic acid (0.1 mg/mL), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application of Cr(VI) detection in environmental water samples was carried out by using the lake water samples, which were collected from Huiling Lake of Anhui University of Technology. The water samples were filtered through the filter paper to remove insoluble components and then spiked with various concentrations of Cr(VI). The fluorescence spectra were recorded when the above solution were added to HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4) containing NH2-mSiO2@CDs. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. To calculate the recovery of Cr(VI), the environmental water samples were added with different concentrations of Cr(VI) standard solutions.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100) was performed to observe the morphology and lattice spacing distance. The Fourier transfer infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained by Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrometer using the KBr pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were performed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7100) equipped with a R928 photomultiplier tube (PMT). Fluorescence decay curves were recorded by Edinburgh FLS1000 spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a NETZSCH STA2500 instrument by heating the sample up to 800 °C at a constant rate of 10 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. All the photographs were taken with HUAWEI Honor10 smartphone.

In our design, the amino group modified mesoporous silica nanosphere was employed as a scaffold to embed CDs for fluorescent assays ( Fig. 1a). Firstly, the mSiO2 was synthesized using a well-established template method. TEOS was utilized as precursors, while CTAB and NaSal served as porous structure-directing agents. After the formation of silica nanospheres, the excess template was removed by reflux in HCl/methanol solution. Subsequently, AEEA, a silane with long-chain amino groups, was select to modify the surface and pore of the mSiO2. Finally, CDs were prepared and anchored on the surface of NH2-mSiO2 by a one-step solvothermal method. Through high temperature and high pressure under solvothermal process, BA and modified amino groups were carbonized together to form fluorescent CDs.

As shown in Fig. 1b, the synthesized mSiO2 exhibited a uniform spherical shape. The particle size distribution obtained by labeling randomly selected samples from TEM image showed that the size of the mSiO2 was 255 nm in diameter (Fig. S1). The pore size is estimated to be 24.2 nm (Fig. S2), it is feasible to embed CDs inside the pore of mesoporous silica. After modification by AEEA, the morphology of the mSiO2 did not change significantly (Fig. 1c). The final product was obtained by solvothermal treatment of amino modified mSiO2 spheres and BA in a Teflon-lined autoclave using ethanol as solvent. Because of the high stability of mSiO2, the mesoporous structure remain unchanged during solvothermal process (Fig. 1d). Besides, the pre-adsorbed BA was carbonized under high temperature to generate CDs in the pore channels.

Since most of the CDs are attached to the pores of mSiO2, the TEM image in Fig. 1d is incapable of showing the CDs accurately. Nevertheless, we use an alkaline solution (2 mol L−1 of NaOH) to etch the silica matrix and release the CDs. It was observed from the TEM image that the obtained CDs are quasi-spherical in shape with the size of 3.9 nm ( Fig. 2a). Meanwhile, the CDs exhibited an obvious lattice spacing distance of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2b), which attributed to the (100) facet of graphite (Jiang et al., 2020). As shown in the full survey XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. 2c), elements of C, N, O, and B existed on the surface of CDs. The signals of Si were also identified due to the incomplete dissolution of mSiO2. The high resolution C 1s spectrum in Fig. S3 demonstrated the chemical bonds of C‒B (283.9 eV), C‒C/C˭C (284.7 eV), C‒N (285.4 eV), and C‒O (285.9 eV) (Shen and Xia, 2014). N 1s spectrum revealed the pyridinic N (398.4 eV), amino N (398.9 eV), and pyrrolic N (400.0 eV) (Wu et al., 2020). Peak fitting of B 1s showed B‒C bond (190.3 eV), B‒N bond (191.0 eV), and B‒O bond (191.9 eV) (Shen et al., 2015). XPS measurements indicated that BA and modified AEEA were carbonized together to generate CDs on the surface of silica matrix. Meanwhile, functional groups such as amino groups are still present on the surface of CDs.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. S4 showed the evolution of surface functional groups in the process of NH2-mSiO2@CDs synthesis. For mesoporous silica, absorption peak at 1090 cm−1 belonged to the Si‒O‒Si stretching vibration, while peaks at 797 and 461 cm−1 were attributed to vibration of Si‒O (Meng et al., 2019). Sharp absorption peaks centered at 2927 and 2851 cm−1 illustrated the C‒H stretching of CTAB. After removing of CTAB and modification of AEEA, the C‒H stretching was attenuated. For NH2-mSiO2@CDs, the B‒O stretching vibration at 1340 cm−1 was detected (Shen and Xia, 2014). Meanwhile, multiple absorption peaks from 780 to 590 cm−1 were corresponded to the benzene ring of BA. These absorbance peaks proved the generation of CDs on the surface of nanospheres. Besides, anchoring of CDs produced two distinguishable bands in the UV–vis absorbance spectra (Fig. 2d), which attributed to the π → π* and n → π* transitions of CDs at 250 and 350 nm, respectively (Zhu et al., 2015).

Finally, we estimated the loading capacity of CDs in silica nanospheres through thermogravimetric analysis (Fig. 2e). For NH2-mSiO2 nanospheres, the decline of weight was due to the evaporation of adsorbed water at the initial stage of heating (below 100 °C). The weight loss took place from 300 to 700 °C was attributed to the decomposition of organic groups on the surface of nanospheres. For NH2-mSiO2@CDs, loading of CDs filled the pores of the silica nanospheres and the weight loss was mainly ascribed to the decomposition of CDs. Compared to the weight loss between NH2-mSiO2 and NH2-mSiO2@CDs, the loading capacity of CDs was approximately 1.45%.

The fluorescent properties of developed NH2-mSiO2@CDs were investigated in detail. Under the excitation of UV light, the CDs gave out bright blue emission centered at 450 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength was 340 nm (Fig. S5). Notably, the emission peaks have not changed with tuning the excitation wavelength in the range of 310–380 nm ( Fig. 3a). To identify that the fluorescence originated from the embedded CDs, the amino grafted mSiO2 was solvothermal treated under the identical condition without BA. The product displayed only a weak emission peak at 430 nm, which is probably induced by the carbonization of amino chain (Fig. S6). Moreover, solvothermal treatment of BA and mSiO2 without amino group modification cannot obtain luminescent nanospheres (Fig. S7). The results confirmed that the amino group modification contributed significantly to the immobilization of CDs on the surface of spheres. Next, we measured the emission spectra of NH2-mSiO2@CDs under different pH conditions. The results showed that the NH2-mSiO2@CDs were highly emissive under the pH range of 3–7 (Fig. 3b). With the protection of silica matrix, the NH2-mSiO2@CDs exhibited excellent photostability. The decline of emission intensity was not observed after the storage for 30 days under ambient temperature and air condition (Fig. 3c).

As shown in Fig. 3d (left), the NH2-mSiO2@CDs dispersed well in water due to their highly hydrophilic surface by amino modification. After centrifugation, the nanospheres were gathered at the bottom of the centrifugal tube, which could be observed by naked eyes under UV light illumination (middle in Fig. 3d). The results showed that the separation of desired CDs could be carried out quickly by a physical way. Traditional time-consuming dialysis for the purification of CDs is avoided by using mesoporous silica as a scaffold. More importantly, the precipitation still gave out obvious blue emission. Benefited from the loading CDs in mSiO2, aggregation caused negligible fluorescence quenching. After ultrasonic treatment, the precipitation dispersed well in water again (right in Fig. 3d). The above results showed that integrating CDs with mSiO2 was a promising method to design fluorophore for convenient sensing platform.

Interestingly, we found that the synthesized NH2-mSiO2@CDs were highly sensitive to Cr(VI), and the fluorescence of anchored CDs can be significantly quenched by Cr(VI). For getting more insight into the quenching behavior, we first examined the influence factor of pH ( Fig. 4a). The NH2-mSiO2@CDs were dispersed in acetic acid buffer solution with different pH values. In the acidic environment (pH = 4), the fluorescence quenching possessed the maximum degree of 63.86% when 10 μM of Cr(VI) were added to 0.075 mg/mL NH2-mSiO2@CDs dispersion. Next, we studied the time-dependent curve of fluorescence intensity at 450 nm in response to Cr(VI) (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence was quenched immediately within 1 min, while the intensity kept stable in the ensuing time. The quick response indicated that CDs in silica matrix offered the possibility to realize favorable simplicity for Cr(VI) sensing. Under the optimized conditions, the fluorescence titration curves were obtained and showed in Fig. 4c. With the concentration of Cr(VI) increased gradually, the blue emission at 450 nm decreased significantly in the concentration range of 0–10.7 μM. The linear range for Cr(VI) was 0.05–9 μM with the relationship of Y = 0.1877X + 0.9918 (R2 = 0.995, Fig. 4d). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 5 nM by 3σ criterion. Compared with the proposed method for Cr(VI) detection in the literature, the sensing performance of the NH2-mSiO2@CDs is more sensitive, providing the feasibility for real sample analysis ( Table 1).

In order to reveal the mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of embedded CDs by Cr(VI), the UV–vis absorption spectra of NH2-mSiO2@CDs and Cr(VI) were measured in pH = 4 buffer ( Fig. 5a). The NH2-mSiO2@CDs exhibited an absorption peak at 350 nm (black line), which corresponded to the optimal fluorescence excitation wavelength (green line). Noticeably, the absorption band of Cr(VI) was obvious and centered at 350 nm, which confirmed the spectral overlap between the fluorescence excitation of CDs and the absorption of Cr(VI). Meanwhile, the absorption of Cr(VI) at 450 nm was very weak, leading to a negligible impact on the emission of fluorophores. On the other hand, we investigated the fluorescence lifetime of NH2-mSiO2@CDs dispersion with and without Cr(VI) quencher (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence lifetime of mSiO2@CDs was 6.4 ns and the introduction of Cr(VI) had a negligible influence on lifetime (6.3 ns), proving that the presence of Cr(VI) did not dynamically quench the energy of the excited state of CDs. Therefore, the inner filter effect (IFE) was the main reason for fluorescence quenching induced by Cr(VI) (Zhang et al., 2019b).

Besides, the electrostatic interaction (EI) between NH2-mSiO2@CDs and the existing species of Cr(VI) improved the efficiency of IFE process (Fig. S8). In the pH = 4 buffer solution, Cr(VI) mainly existed in the form of HCrO4 − with negative charges (Zhao et al., 2005; Hu et al., 2005). The zeta potential of NH2-mSiO2@CDs was 32.4 mV due to the protonation of amino group in acidic solution (Fig. S9). Thus, the nanospheres were functionalized with positive surface charges. Strong electrostatic interaction resulted in the accumulation of HCrO4 − on the surfaces of nanospheres. Consequently, the excitation of CDs in pore channels was severely restricted by IFE.

To confirm the selectivity of the NH2-mSiO2@CDs sensor towards Cr(VI), interfering metal ions were used for the comparative experiments in acetic acid buffer at pH = 4, including Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Cr(III), Cd2+, Ni2+, and Pb2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 6, upon the addition of test ions, only Cr(VI) and Cu2+ lead to a noticeable fluorescence intensity quenching of NH2-mSiO2@CDs. Particularly, Cr(III) can barely induce remarkable fluorescence quenching, so the probe is available for Cr(VI) detection distinguishing from Cr(III). However, Cu2+ ions caused some interference, which may be attributed to the specific interaction of Cu2+ with amino groups on the surface of NH2-mSiO2@CDs nanospheres (Liu et al., 2014, 2017a). Fortunately, suitable masking agents can take away this issue. Thiourea (Th), a suitable masking agent, has strong coordination ability with Cu2+ to form a stable complex (Behpour et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-mSiO2@CDs quenched slightly by Cu2+ in the simultaneous presence of Th masking, while the fluorescence signal was significantly quenched after adding 10 μM of Cr(VI). The results indicated that Th could be used as a masking agent to eliminate the influence of Cu2+ on Cr(VI) detection. To verify the interference of other negatively charged substances, comparative experiments were performed using SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Cl−, NO3 −, and humic acid (Fig. S11). The emissions of NH2-mSiO2@CDs were also effectively quenched by Cr(VI) in the presence of these interfering species. Therefore, the interference caused by these ions is almost negligible. The interference experiment clearly elucidated the superior selectivity of NH2-mSiO2@CDs sensor towards Cr(VI) ions, further proving the potential application for the real samples.

In addition, the toxicity of the developed nanospheres was estimated by MTT assay method. A549 cell was selected as model and different concentrations of the NH2-mSiO2@CDs dispersion were added for co-culture. As shown in Fig. S12, when the concentration reached to 2 mg/mL (which is 26.6 times higher than that used for Cr(VI) detection), cell viability was still as high as 94.25%. The reduced toxicity is the result of the joint action of low toxicity CDs and biocompatible silica matrix.

Fascinating analytical characteristics of the NH2-mSiO2@CDs based fluorescent sensing platform promoted us to further evaluate the detection performance in lake water samples. In real water samples, the fluorescence intensity of NH2-mSiO2@CDs nanospheres decreased with increasing the concentration of Cr(VI). Meanwhile, a good linear relationship was obtained from 0.5 to 10 μM (Fig. S13). The recovery of the standard addition method is in the range of 99.8–105.2% ( Table 2). This fluorescent response can also be observed by the naked eyes, indicating the present NH2-mSiO2@CDs can be an excellent sensor for the direct determination of Cr(VI) for real samples (Fig. S14).

In conclusion, we successfully designed a novel fluorescent CDs embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres for the detection of the environmental pollutant Cr(VI). Through the amino modification on the surface of silica matrix, the NH2-mSiO2@CDs were positively charged in the acidic buffer. Therefore, strong electrostatic interaction occurred between NH2-mSiO2@CDs and the negatively charged Cr(VI). Due to the overlap between the excitation of NH2-mSiO2@CDs and the absorption of Cr(VI), an efficient inner filter effect system was established, which led to the fluorescence quenching. This fluorescent sensor exhibits many unique advantages, such as excellent photostability, low biological toxicity, easy to dispersion and separation. Moreover, we expect that a multi-purpose sensing platform can be designed through appropriate surface modification and multiple response mechanisms using mSiO2@CDs nanohybrid.

Meilin Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing-original draft. Taotao Li: Visualization, Resources. Cheng Zhang: Investigation, Writing-original draft, Funding acquisition. Yu Zheng: Formal analysis, Resources. Chenqing Wu: Resources. Jian Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Kui Zhang: Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhongping Zhang: Writing-original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675158 and 21976002), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1908085MB41).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.125699.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
